perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” means ISCA’s information comprising all data and information relating to the business , technical , and other operations of ISCA , and all other information of ISCA whatsoever obtained by Vendor pursuant to, by virtue of, or in the course of negotiating or performing the Services or an agreement for the Services , including where applicable (without any lim itation): (a) all technical information, data, databases, source codes, object codes, know -how, processes, formulae, designs, drawings, documents, specifications, manuals, inventions, images, and all information comprised in and/or concerning equipment, system s, research, prototypes, samples, products, or services; (b) all of ISCA’s plans, models, proposals, ideas, functionalities, workflow, work processes, network and system information, including those relating to the Services; (c) all marketing, sales, business, fi nancial, operational, commercial, and human resource information , including financial data and business plans ; (d) all contractual arrangements, forecasts, accounting and tax records, strategies, models, product and service information; (e) all ISCA IPR; ISCA Non -Disclosure Agreement (f) all of Vendor’s proposals to ISCA for the Services, and ISCA’s requirements, requests, instructions, and specifications to Vendor for the Services; (g) all communication between Vendor and ISCA; (h) all customer, client , and employee information and personal data ; (i) any other proprietary information ; and (j) all other information, data, experience and expertise, materials and diskettes stamped or marked as "confidential" of whatever kind , whether written, electronic or in oral form, which is either directly or indirectly dis closed to the Vendor or received by the Vendor from ISCA and/or its employees, advisors or consultants, whether or not such information belongs to ISCA , or to a third party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” means ISCA’s information comprising all data and information relating to the business , technical , and other operations of ISCA , and all other information of ISCA whatsoever obtained by Vendor pursuant to, by virtue of, or in the course of negotiating or performing the Services or an agreement for the Services , including where applicable (without any lim itation): (a) all technical information, data, databases, source codes, object codes, know -how, processes, formulae, designs, drawings, documents, specifications, manuals, inventions, images, and all information comprised in and/or concerning equipment, system s, research, prototypes, samples, products, or services; (b) all of ISCA’s plans, models, proposals, ideas, functionalities, workflow, work processes, network and system information, including those relating to the Services; (c) all marketing, sales, business, fi nancial, operational, commercial, and human resource information , including financial data and business plans ; (d) all contractual arrangements, forecasts, accounting and tax records, strategies, models, product and service information; (e) all ISCA IPR; ISCA Non -Disclosure Agreement (f) only Vendor’s final proposals to ISCA for the Services that have been approved in writing by ISCA, and ISCA’s requirements, requests, instructions, and specifications to Vendor for the Services; (g) all communication between Vendor and ISCA if marked confidential; (h) aggregated customer, client , and employee information and personal data, excluding individual identifiable records; (i) only proprietary information marked explicitly as such; and (j) all other information, data, experience and expertise, materials and diskettes stamped or marked as "confidential" of whatever kind , whether written, electronic or in oral form, which is either directly or indirectly dis closed to the Vendor or received by the Vendor from ISCA and/or its employees, advisors or consultants, whether or not such information belongs to ISCA , or to a third party.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces in-text contradiction by narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Specifically, (f) is changed to include 'only Vendor’s final proposals to ISCA for the Services that have been approved in writing by ISCA', contradicting the broader scope previously included. Similarly, changes to (g) now require communications to be 'marked confidential', (h) excludes 'individual identifiable records', and (i) demands that 'proprietary information marked explicitly as such'. All these contradict the original clause that includes ‘all data and information relating to the business’ and 'all other information of ISCA whatsoever', creating uncertainty about which definition applies. The new limitations contradict the original broader definition of Confidential Information, especially 2.1 The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confidential and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confiden tial and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' compared to the original text. Specifically, limitations are introduced regarding Vendor proposals, communication between Vendor and ISCA, customer data, and proprietary information. These limitations directly contradict clause 2.1, which states that *any part or the whole of any Confidential Information* must be kept confidential. The altered definition introduces inconsistency about which definition applies, creating a strong in-text contradiction that impacts the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confiden tial and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confidential, but only to the extent reasonably possible, and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information. Vendor shall use reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction. Initially, clause 2.1 places a strict obligation on the Vendor to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information. The modified clause weakens this obligation by adding 'but only to the extent reasonably possible' and that 'Vendor shall use reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality.' This contradicts clause 9, 'Equitable Relief', which states that ISCA is entitled to an injunction for any breach of the Agreement. This contradiction arises because the 'reasonable efforts' clause weakens the initial stringent confidentiality obligation, making enforcement of the 'Equitable Relief' clause more difficult. It creates uncertainty regarding the degree of confidentiality the vendor is expected to uphold, directly contradicting the implication of strict confidentiality intended by the original Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Equitable Relief The Parties agree that in the event of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the Vendor , ISCA shall be entitled to obtain equitable relief, including without any limitation, an injunction and/or specific performance, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the Vendor's obligation to maintain confidentiality by adding 'but only to the extent reasonably possible' and 'Vendor shall use reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality.' This directly contradicts clause 9, 'Equitable Relief,' which entitles ISCA to an injunction for any breach of the Agreement. The original text imposed a strict obligation, while the changed text introduces a degree of flexibility that makes enforcing the 'Equitable Relief' clause more difficult, creating a strong in-text contradiction. This is an instance of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 The Vendor shall: (1) not without the prior written consent of ISCA copy, reproduce, distribute , disclose , or allow access to any Confidential In formation to any person, other than those employees who are directly concerned with fulfilling the Limited Purpose (subject to compliance with Clause 3) and purely on a “need to know” basis in furtherance of the Limited Purpose (“Authorised Employees ”), and shall not use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Limited Purpose ;
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 The Vendor shall: (1) not without the prior written consent of ISCA copy, reproduce, distribute , disclose , or allow access to any Confidential In formation to any person, other than those employees who are directly concerned with fulfilling the Limited Purpose (subject to compliance with Clause 3) and purely on a “need to know” basis in furtherance of the Limited Purpose (“Authorised Employees ”), and shall not use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Limited Purpose, except if such use demonstrably benefits the Vendor and does not cause direct harm to ISCA ;
perturbation.explanation: The change adds an exception to the confidentiality obligation: 'except if such use demonstrably benefits the Vendor and does not cause direct harm to ISCA.' This contradicts the initial restriction that the Vendor 'shall not use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Limited Purpose.' This conflict raises ambiguity and creates in-text contradiction, because it allows the vendor to potentially use confidential information if certain criteria are met (benefits the vendor, doesn't harm ISCA), which could potentially conflict with section 7 'Acknowledgements', subsection 1: ‘it does not acquire by implication or otherwise any right, title or interest i n or to the Confidential Information and ISCA IPR, including any licence.’
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Acknowledgements Vendor acknowledges and agrees that: (1) it does not acquire by implication or otherwise any right, title or interest i n or to the Confidential Information and ISCA IPR, including any licence; (2) nothing in this Agreement nor in any discussions nor disclosures made pursuant to this Agreement shall be deemed a commitment to disclose any information to the Vendor or to engage i n any business relationship, contract or future dealing with the Vendor ; (3) nothing herein shall create or be deemed to create a partnership or joint venture or relationship of principal and agent or any manner of business relationship whatsoever between th e Parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text of section 2.2 strictly prohibits the Vendor from using Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Limited Purpose. The changed text introduces an exception allowing such use if it benefits the Vendor and doesn't directly harm ISCA. This directly contradicts section 7(1), which states that the Vendor 'does not acquire by implication or otherwise any right, title or interest i n or to the Confidential Information and ISCA IPR, including any licence'. By allowing the vendor to use the information for their benefit, even if it doesn't harm ISCA, implies they are gaining a right or interest in the Confidential Information, which section 7(1) explicitly denies. This creates a strong in-text contradiction under the definition of inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 In the event that the Parties do not execute any a greement for the Services or upon demand by ISCA , the Vendor undertakes to forthwith: (1) return all Confidential Information (including all originals and copies) to ISCA ; or (2) where (1) is not possible, at ISCA’s option , destroy, erase, delete, or securely dispose of such Confidential Information, and to furnish ISCA with a written confirmation , certifying that no copies of the Confidential Information have been made, reproduced, retained , or distributed by itself, its employees , or any third parties , without prejudice to the obligations of confidentiality hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 In the event that the Parties do not execute any a greement for the Services or upon demand by ISCA, unless the Vendor has independently developed equivalent information without reference to ISCA's Confidential Information, the Vendor undertakes to forthwith: (1) return all Confidential Information (including all originals and copies) to ISCA; or (2) where (1) is not possible, at ISCA’s option, destroy, erase, delete, or securely dispose of such Confidential Information, and to furnish ISCA with a written confirmation, certifying that no copies of the Confidential Information have been made, reproduced, retained, or distributed by itself, its employees, or any third parties, without prejudice to the obligations of confidentiality hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a condition where the Vendor doesn't need to return or destroy confidential information if it has 'independently developed equivalent information without reference to ISCA's Confidential Information'. This directly contradicts the obligation in Clause 2.3(1) to 'return all Confidential Information (including all originals and copies) to ISCA' upon demand or failure to execute an agreement. If the Vendor claims to have independently developed equivalent information, it creates uncertainty and potential disputes regarding whether the vendor is obligated to return or destroy the Confidential Information, which is contradictory to clause 2.1 'The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confidential and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confiden tial and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a condition where the Vendor doesn't need to return or destroy confidential information if it has independently developed equivalent information. This directly contradicts Clause 2.1, which states 'The Vendor hereby agrees to maintain as confidential and agrees not to use except for the Limited Purpose, any part or the whole of any Confidential Information.' The addition creates uncertainty and potential disputes, violating the Vendor's obligation to maintain confidentiality, thus creating an inconsistency and falling under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 The Vendor shall, before any permitted disclosures are made to any employees pursuant to Clause 2.2(1) , inform such employees of the Vendor ’s obligations under this Agreement and bind such employees to observe the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Vendor shall, make reasonable efforts, before any permitted disclosures are made to any employees pursuant to Clause 2.2(1) , inform such employees of the Vendor ’s obligations under this Agreement and bind such employees to observe the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification of the phrase 'Vendor shall' to 'Vendor shall, make reasonable efforts' introduces a contradiction within the agreement. The change weakens the initial obligation by adding the element of 'reasonable efforts'. This contradicts the strict compliance requirement stated in 3.2, 'For the avoidance of doubt, the Vendor shall be responsible for ensuring its employees’ strict compliance with the terms of this Agreement.'. If 'reasonable efforts' are made but employees still don't comply, it leads to an uncertain situation, and this creates conflict and questions whether the Vendor has fulfilled its obligation under this agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 For the avoidance of doubt, the Vendor shall be responsible for ensuring its employees’ strict compliance with the terms of this Agreement. Any non -compliance with the terms of this Agreement by an employee of the Vendor shall be deemed a breach of this Agreement by Vendor .
perturbation.justification: The original text states a firm obligation: 'The Vendor shall inform... and bind such employees.' The changed text weakens this obligation to 'The Vendor shall, make reasonable efforts, before any permitted disclosures are made to any employees pursuant to Clause 2.2(1) , inform such employees of the Vendor’s obligations under this Agreement and bind such employees to observe the terms of this Agreement.' This introduces a direct conflict with Clause 3.2, which mandates 'strict compliance' and holds the Vendor responsible for any employee non-compliance. The 'reasonable efforts' language creates a loophole where the Vendor might not ensure strict compliance, directly contradicting the requirement in 3.2. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Third Party Disclosure In the event that the Vendor requires the assistance of any third party other than employees of the Vendor , to whom disclosure of any Confidential Information is necessary, the Vendor shall first seek ISCA ’s written approval of such third party and thereafter , prior to any disclosure to that third party, obtain from that third party a duly binding non-disclosure agreement on terms at least as binding upon that third party as the Vendor is bound to ISCA hereunder which terms will be subject to ISCA ’s prior written approval .
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Third Party Disclosure In the event that the Vendor requires the assistance of any third party other than employees of the Vendor, to whom disclosure of any Confidential Information is necessary, the Vendor shall notify ISCA of such requirement, but ISCA's approval is not required. The Vendor shall obtain from that third party a non-disclosure agreement on terms that the Vendor deems reasonable, which terms will not be subject to ISCA’s approval.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text directly contradicts the original requirement for ISCA's written approval before disclosing Confidential Information to a third party. Initially, Clause 4 mandates that the Vendor 'shall first seek ISCA’s written approval' and also ensure the third-party NDA terms 'will be subject to ISCA’s prior written approval.' However, the modified clause states 'ISCA's approval is not required' and 'which terms will not be subject to ISCA’s approval,' creating an in-text contradiction because it empowers the Vendor to decide on third-party disclosures and NDA terms independently without ISCA's oversight. This also creates a contradictory scenario against the clause where 'Vendor shall at all times comply with the provisions of the PDPA and not cause ISCA t o be in breach of its obligations under the PDPA, including in relation to Personal Data of Customers and ISCA’s employees.' since Vendor can disclose to 3rd parties without ISCA's approval.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.2 Vendor shall at all times comply with the provisions of the PDPA and not cause ISCA t o be in breach of its obligations under the PDPA, including in relation to Personal Data of Customers and ISCA’s employees.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Clause 4 directly contradicts Clause 6.2. The original Clause 4 required ISCA's written approval for third-party disclosures and NDA terms. The changed Clause 4 removes this requirement, stating 'ISCA's approval is not required' and 'which terms will not be subject to ISCA’s approval.' This contradicts Clause 6.2, which mandates that the Vendor shall at all times comply with the provisions of the PDPA and not cause ISCA to be in breach of its obligations under the PDPA. By removing ISCA's oversight on third-party disclosures, the Vendor could potentially breach PDPA obligations without ISCA's knowledge or control, directly conflicting with Clause 6.2.
